problem_summary: 2010 MCM Problem B (discrete) — the "Crime Busting" geographic-profiling problem. Given the spatial coordinates and temporal ordering of a violent serial criminal's past crimes, build a model that predicts WHERE the offender will strike next, output as a likelihood surface over a 2D map, plus a way to express confidence in the prediction. Team 7507's framing: rather than predict the criminal's home/anchor point (the well-trodden problem in the literature), they predict the next CRIME location by first estimating anchor point(s) and then projecting the offender's empirical radial crime pattern back outward from those anchors.
models_used: Single-anchor 'Centroid Method': anchor point = unweighted centroid (center of mass) of all prior crime coordinates

'Likelihood crater' via Gamma distribution: map each crime to its radial distance r_i = ||x_i - anchor||_2 from the anchor, fit a Gamma(k, theta) to the set {r_i} by Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MATLAB gamfit), then evaluate the fitted Gamma pdf at every grid cell's radius to build a 2D likelihood surface, normalized to integrate to 1

Gamma chosen deliberately as a flexible family that interpolates between the two competing criminological theories: a 'buffer zone' annulus (shifted-Gaussian shape, large k) and a monotonically decaying-exponential 'distance-decay' pattern (k≈1) — letting the data decide which holds

Temporal trend adjustment: shift the random variable by mean step change in radius, X-tilde = X + mean(Delta r) where Delta r = r_n - r_(n-1), so E[X-tilde] = E[X] + mean(Delta r) — captures an offender drifting inward/outward over time
Multiple-anchor 'Cluster Method': agglomerative hierarchical clustering (dendrogram) on Euclidean inter-point distances, with centroid-linkage merging, constrained to 2-4 clusters
Silhouette coefficient (Rousseeuw) to auto-select the optimal cluster count k in {2,3,4} — averaging silhouette over non-singleton points and maximizing; removes the need to set k by hand
Per-cluster likelihood craters (Gamma per cluster centroid), with singleton clusters treated as outliers modeled by a single Gaussian rather than an anchor

Linear combination of per-cluster surfaces with two-factor weighting: (a) size weight = points-in-cluster / total points, and (b) recency weight = average temporal index of cluster events / final index; weighted surfaces summed and re-normalized

Effectiveness multiplier kappa: ratio of resource (likelihood) allocated at the TRUE next-crime location under model 1 vs model 2; the 'standard' kappa_s compares the model against a flat/uniform distribution (random guess = the 'psychic and hairfinder' baseline)

Combined meta-model / model selector: leave-last-out backtesting — for M = 4..N-1 use first M crimes to predict crime M+1, compute kappa for both methods, declare a method 'invalid' if >50% of its kappa values fall below 1, otherwise pick the method with the higher running-mean kappa; the running mean kappa_s-bar doubles as a self-reported confidence score
key_techniques: Reframing the assigned problem: predict next-strike location by piggybacking on the better-researched anchor-point estimation problem, then projecting the radial crime distribution outward
Reducing 2D spatial data to a 1D radial distance distribution about an anchor, making MLE fitting tractable and the buffer-zone/distance-decay debate a single-parameter question
Using the Gamma family specifically because its two free parameters span BOTH rival domain theories — a single model that empirically arbitrates between them

Parameter minimization as a design principle: every parameter is either fit from data (Gamma k, theta via MLE; cluster count via silhouette) or justified by published empirical studies; the team explicitly refuses to add free 'tuning knobs' (e.g. elliptical/anisotropic extensions) they cannot calibrate

Solving the data-scarcity problem with 'pixel point analysis': digitizing scatter-plot figures from criminology papers (Rossmo PhD thesis, LeBeau rape-case study) back into numeric coordinates — and explicitly rejecting simulated data because simulating from a journey-to-crime model and then testing a journey-to-crime predictor on it is circular and self-confirming

Blind testing protocol: model built and debugged on a separate development set, then evaluated leave-last-out on held-out offenders; honestly reports a failure case (Offender A, kappa_s = 0.38 then 0.001) instead of hiding it
A custom, decision-theoretic success metric (kappa / effectiveness multiplier) grounded in the actual end-use: optimal allocation of finite police resources proportional to predicted likelihood

Metric robustness engineering: normalizing the comparison area by building a square of side = max inter-crime distance centered at centroid, then scaling x3, so kappa_s is comparable across data sets with different geographic spread (borrowed from Canter 2000)
Backtesting-as-validation AND backtesting-as-confidence: the same leave-one-out sweep both selects the model and produces a numeric confidence estimate
why_it_won: "Judges reward papers that connect rigorous mathematics to honest, well-scoped science, and this paper does both unusually well. (1) Strong literature grounding — it reads like real quantitative criminology, citing Rossmo, Canter & Larkin, LeBeau, silhouette/clustering sources, and building every assumption on empirical findings rather than convenience. (2) A genuinely original, problem-fitted metric (the effectiveness multiplier kappa) derived from the real decision the police face — resource allocation — not a generic error norm. (3) Intellectual honesty that judges love: it openly fails on Offender A, states results are NOT statistically significant given only 3 test sets, admits the 'intuitive cop' will likely beat the model, and argues that anchor-point prediction may be a better practice than the very next-strike prediction it was asked to do. (4) Disciplined parameter minimization — 'the model contains no arbitrary parameters' is a clean, defensible thesis. (5) A clever, transparent data-acquisition story (pixel-point digitization of published figures, with a principled rejection of circular simulation) that turns a real obstacle into a strength. (6) A self-aware combined model that backtests itself, selects the better method, AND emits a confidence number. The whole package is sophisticated yet candid — exactly the maturity an Outstanding committee looks for."
section_highlights: "The writing structure is a model worth emulating. Memorable, framed section titles do real work: 'Metrics of Success: The Psychic and the Hairfinder' anchors the entire evaluation philosophy in a vivid Rossmo anecdote (a fake psychic + a 'hairfinder' who found the body by luck) that gives the team a concrete, quotable baseline — 'we must beat the psychic and the hairfinder' = beat random chance. The Background section is a true mini literature review that motivates every modeling choice. Assumptions are not a bulleted afterthought but a multi-page argued case (urbanization -> dense targets + L2 metric + completeness, justified individually and tied to a metric-space formalization). Each method gets its own flowchart (Figures 2 and 8), an Algorithm subsection, a Results-and-Analysis subsection, and an explicit Extensions subsection that proposes refinements AND explains why they were deliberately NOT included (no data to calibrate them). Heavy use of per-offender heat maps and 3D surface 'crater' plots makes the abstract likelihood surfaces tangible. There are TWO summaries — the contest summary sheet and a separate plain-language Executive Summary with explicit Strengths/Weaknesses/Inputs/Assumptions/Method/Recommendations aimed squarely at a police chief. Notation is consistent (kappa, kappa_s, kappa_s-bar). The narrative voice is confident but self-critical throughout."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Severe data poverty: only 7 digitized sequences for 3 offenders, all serial rapists, with just 3 used as the held-out test set — the team itself repeatedly states results are not statistically significant and not generalizable

Validation circularity risk acknowledged but not fully escaped: the model assumes journey-to-crime/anchor-point structure and is tested on data the original criminologists pre-selected and pre-cleaned to exhibit exactly that structure
Pixel-point digitization of published scatter plots introduces measurement error and is not reproducible/auditable in the way a real police dataset would be

Radially symmetric Gamma crater cannot capture angular asymmetry (e.g. Offender B's horizontal elongation); the proposed elliptical/anisotropic/'gravity'-kernel fixes were dropped for lack of calibration data, leaving a known model-fit gap

The cluster method 'gambles' — sharper, taller craters that exclude outliers raise kappa when right but are catastrophically wrong on outlier final crimes (Offender A kappa_s collapses to 0.001); the model has no mechanism to hedge this boldness

kappa_s is not area-invariant — it can be inflated arbitrarily by padding empty map area; the x3-square normalization only approximately fixes comparability across data sets and depends on an unknowable true 'active region'
Confidence estimate kappa_s-bar is demonstrably unreliable in the presence of a late behavioral shift (it predicted high confidence for Offender A right before total failure)

Silhouette-based cluster selection is biased to increase with k, forcing an ad hoc cap at 4 clusters; it also cannot evaluate the single-cluster (k=1) case at all, so the method can never fall back to one anchor even when warranted

By the team's own admission the model likely loses to an 'intuitive cop' and ignores all underlying map semantics (lakes, red-light districts, parole-officer zones), so it is an augmentation tool, not a standalone predictor
Temporal information is deliberately reduced to mere ordering — it cannot predict WHEN, and the linear mean-Delta-r trend term is a crude first-order extrapolation
reusable_patterns: Design a bespoke success metric from the decision the stakeholder actually faces (resource allocation -> effectiveness multiplier), then benchmark against an explicit, vivid baseline ('beat the psychic and the hairfinder' = beat uniform/random)
Pick a flexible distribution family (Gamma here) precisely because its parameters span competing domain theories, then let MLE-on-the-data choose which theory holds — avoids picking sides arbitrarily
Reduce a hard 2D/high-D spatial problem to a 1D radial distribution about a learned center to make fitting tractable
Use leave-last-out / leave-one-out backtesting both to SELECT between competing models and to PRODUCE a self-reported confidence score from the same sweep
Auto-select the number of clusters with the silhouette coefficient instead of hard-coding k; pair hierarchical clustering + silhouette as a parameter-free grouping module
Adopt 'minimize free parameters' as an explicit, stated design thesis — fit what you can, cite literature for the rest, and openly decline extensions you cannot calibrate (and say why)
When real data is scarce, recover it from published figures via pixel/coordinate digitization, and explicitly argue against synthetic data when it would create circular self-validation
Run a genuine blind test (build/debug on one set, evaluate on a held-out set) and report failure cases candidly with diagnosis
Normalize your evaluation metric for scale/area so scores are comparable across heterogeneous instances (the x3-bounding-square trick)
Structure each model identically — flowchart, algorithm, results, and a dedicated 'Extensions' section that states what you left out and the reason — to telegraph rigor and self-awareness to judges
Write two summaries: the contest summary sheet plus a stakeholder-facing Executive Summary with bolded Strengths/Weaknesses/Inputs/Assumptions/Recommendations

Position the model honestly relative to the human expert ('we augment, not replace, the intuitive cop') and even question whether the assigned objective is the right one — a maturity signal judges reward
